meta.purpose: Judgment layer for short-video subtitles: what makes a subtitle not just correct but audience-appropriate, rhythm-aware, and brand-aligned.

axioms.one_sentence: Subtitle breaks must serve the emotional rhythm of the content, not just character-count rules.
axioms.full_statement: When splitting long subtitles, prioritize the speaker's pacing, emotional beats, and natural pauses over mechanical line-length limits. A technically short line that breaks mid-emotion is worse than a slightly long line that preserves the emotional flow.
axioms.why: Rigid character-count splitting produces subtitles that are easy to read but dead to the rhythm. Viewers feel the disconnect even if they can't name it.
applies_when: the content is emotional/rhythmic (storytelling, commentary, vlogs)
the speaker's pacing is part of the message
does_not_apply_when: the content is purely informational (tutorials, news)
auto-captioning for accessibility without creative intent
axioms.failure_risk: Mechanically chopped subtitles that read correctly but feel wrong — the viewer trusts the content less.

axioms.one_sentence: Subtitle tone and density must adapt to the target audience, not default to universal readability.
axioms.full_statement: A subtitle track for teenagers on Douyin needs different line length, vocabulary level, and reading speed than a subtitle track for senior audiences on WeChat Channels. Defaulting to one-size-fits-all readability means serving no audience well.
axioms.why: Generic subtitles feel off-brand because they ignore the audience's reading habits, attention span, and platform norms.
applies_when: the content targets a specific audience segment
the platform has known audience demographics
does_not_apply_when: the content is designed for maximum reach across all audiences
the platform provides no audience data
axioms.failure_risk: Subtitles that are technically readable but contextually alienating — seniors get overwhelmed, teenagers get bored.

axioms.one_sentence: When clarity and tone conflict, preserve the tone that matches the account's brand voice.
axioms.full_statement: Ambiguous phrases in speech can often be clarified by rewriting. But rewriting for clarity can strip the speaker's personality — their humor, their warmth, their authority. For branded content, losing voice is more damaging than losing perfect clarity.
axioms.why: Over-clarified subtitles feel like they were written by a committee. The audience came for the speaker's voice, not a textbook.
applies_when: the content is personality-driven (influencer, thought leader, brand account)
the speaker has a distinctive voice
does_not_apply_when: the content is instructional where clarity is paramount
the speaker's phrasing could cause genuine misunderstanding
axioms.failure_risk: Sanitized subtitles that are perfectly clear but no longer sound like the person who made the video.

axioms.one_sentence: Anxiety-driving language in subtitles undermines trust, even if the underlying content is accurate.
axioms.full_statement: Phrases like 'you're making a huge mistake,' 'most people get this wrong,' or 'if you don't do this now' may increase short-term engagement but erode long-term credibility. Trust-building accounts avoid manufactured urgency in subtitles.
axioms.why: Viewers subconsciously associate the account with the emotional state the subtitles induce. Anxiety = untrustworthy source.
applies_when: the content discusses health, finance, career, parenting, or any high-stakes topic
the account positions itself as a trusted advisor
does_not_apply_when: the content is entertainment or satire
genuine urgency exists (breaking news, safety alerts)
axioms.failure_risk: High engagement in the short term, eroded credibility in the long term. The account becomes known for fear-mongering.

ontology.one_sentence: Reading pace is cognitive load per screen, not words-per-minute.
ontology.essence: Subtitle reading pace is not words-per-minute — it's the cognitive load per screen. A short line that introduces a new concept may need more screen time than a long line of familiar words.
ontology.boundary: Reading pace is not typing speed. It's about how fast the viewer processes meaning, not characters.
ontology.trigger_signal: viewer re-reads, viewer pauses video, viewer comments 'too fast'

ontology.one_sentence: An emotional beat is a feeling-carrying moment in delivery — subtitle breaks must respect it.
ontology.essence: An emotional beat is a moment in the speaker's delivery that carries feeling — a pause, a laugh, a shift in tone. Subtitle breaks that ignore emotional beats make the content feel flat.
ontology.boundary: An emotional beat is not the same as a sentence boundary. A speaker may pause mid-sentence for effect.
ontology.trigger_signal: speaker pauses, tone shifts, laughter, sigh, emphasis word

stances: Subtitles are editorial decisions, not transcription. Every line break is a creative choice.
Serve the audience, not the algorithm. Engagement at the cost of trust is failure.
When in doubt, preserve the speaker's voice over perfect grammar.
